banner: Week 34 · Prove It — The Six Trig Ratios
essential_question: Can you defend WHY csc θ must equal 1/sin θ — not just recite the reciprocal pairs — and compute all six ratios cold?
objective: Justify the three reciprocal relationships and the identity sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 from the definitions of the six ratios, review the week's Math-U-See practice, and pass the final mastery checkpoint of the course.

content.prompt: Without looking, sketch a right triangle, label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to θ, and write from memory all six ratio names paired with their reciprocals (sin↔csc, cos↔sec, tan↔cot). Then check against your concept note.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 30A & 30B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 30A/30B. We check the tricky ones: reducing every ratio all the way (10/6 must become 5/3, not stay as a decimal), the problems where θ moves to the OTHER acute vertex of the same triangle so opposite and adjacent trade places, and the sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 fill-in-the-blank on both worksheets. Fix any you missed and note WHY.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: csc θ must equal 1/sin θ. Argue it directly from the definitions — sin θ = opp/hyp and csc θ = hyp/opp are the same two numbers, one on top of the other flipped — so multiplying them always collapses to 1.

Claim 2: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 for EVERY right triangle, no matter how big or small. Argue it from the Pythagorean theorem: sin²θ + cos²θ = (opp/hyp)² + (adj/hyp)² = (opp² + adj²)/hyp², and opp² + adj² = hyp² is exactly the Pythagorean theorem — so the fraction is always hyp²/hyp² = 1.

Claim 3: if you rotate the same right triangle so θ sits at the OTHER acute vertex, sin θ and cos θ swap values (the old adjacent becomes the new opposite, and vice versa) — but sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 still holds at the new vertex too. Argue why that identity survives the swap.

content.preview: This completes all 30 lessons of Math-U-See Geometry — next is the year-end final exam and the year-end capstone project, not another weekly lesson.

success_criteria.text: I can justify why each reciprocal pair (sin/csc, cos/sec, tan/cot) always multiplies to 1
I can derive sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 from the Pythagorean theorem and explain why it holds for any right triangle
I can compute all six trigonometric ratios, reduced, for a given right triangle without help

compass.note: Curriculum/Geometry/The Salas Compass/2026-W34 The Six Trig Ratios.md

questions.prompt: The reciprocal of cos θ is called…
questions.explain: sec θ (secant) = hyp/adj, exactly cos θ = adj/hyp flipped upside down.

questions.prompt: A right triangle has legs 3 and 4 and hypotenuse 5. θ sits at the vertex between the leg of 4 and the hypotenuse, so the leg of 3 is opposite θ and the leg of 4 is adjacent to θ. Which ratio equals 4/3?
questions.explain: cot θ = adj/opp = 4/3. tan θ would be opp/adj = 3/4, the reciprocal of this answer.

questions.prompt: For that same 3-4-5 triangle (opposite = 3, adjacent = 4, hypotenuse = 5), find all six trigonometric ratios for θ. Reduce every fraction.
questions.answer: sin θ = 3/5; cos θ = 4/5; tan θ = 3/4; csc θ = 5/3; sec θ = 5/4; cot θ = 4/3.
questions.explain: 3-4-5 is already in lowest terms with no common factor, so every ratio built from it is already reduced.

questions.prompt: Using the baseline (adjacent), height-above-eye (opposite), and hypotenuse you measured and computed in your Build session, recompute sin θ and cos θ as decimals (three places), then confirm sin²θ + cos²θ ≈ 1 using your own real numbers.
questions.answer: Answers vary by student's own measurements, e.g. sin θ ≈ 0.530, cos θ ≈ 0.848, and 0.530² + 0.848² ≈ 0.281 + 0.719 = 1.000.
questions.explain: Rubric: correctly recomputes sin θ and cos θ from their own recorded sides (1 pt) + correctly squares and adds to confirm ≈ 1, allowing for measurement rounding (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Explain, from the definitions (not just 'it's the rule'), why csc θ must always equal 1/sin θ.
questions.answer: sin θ = opp/hyp and csc θ = hyp/opp are the same two side lengths, just with the numerator and denominator swapped. Any fraction times its swapped version equals 1 — (opp/hyp) × (hyp/opp) = (opp×hyp)/(hyp×opp) = 1 — so csc θ is exactly 1 ÷ sin θ.
questions.explain: Full credit connects the reciprocal to the actual side-length fractions, not just to a memorized flipping rule.

questions.prompt: Fill in the blank exactly as on your MUS worksheet: sin²θ + cos²θ = ______. This identity is really the ______ expressed with trigonometric ratios.
questions.answer: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1. This identity is really the Pythagorean theorem expressed with trigonometric ratios.
questions.explain: Dividing a² + b² = c² through by c² turns it into (a/c)² + (b/c)² = 1, which is sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 once a/c and b/c are named sin θ and cos θ.

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 30 — and on the whole trig arc, Lessons 29-30 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) — and name the one ratio, of all six, you'd most want to drill again before the year-end final.
